description:
You are the system administrator responsible for log rotation and quick-look reporting on a small fleet of servers.  
Inside the workspace you will find a directory that already contains raw log/metric data:

    /home/user/server_logs/

Initial content of that directory:
    • access.log          – 12 lines of classic Apache combined-format traffic logs  
    • error.log           – 10 lines of mixed-level Java-style application logs  
    • usage.csv           – comma-separated resource metrics from three servers  

Your assignment is to use *only* standard text-processing tools that exist in a default Linux environment (e.g. awk, sed, sort, uniq, cut, etc.) to mine these three source files and build a concise daily “ops digest”.  All of the deliverables must be placed in a brand-new directory called

    /home/user/server_logs/summary/

The summary directory must be created by the agent if it is not already present.  
Four separate artefacts have to be produced and their exact names, locations and formats **will be unit-tested verbatim**:

1. /home/user/server_logs/summary/access_summary.txt  
   • Purpose : show how many HTTP requests each unique source IP made.  
   • Required ordering : numerically descending on the request count; if two IPs share the same count, sort them lexicographically in ascending IP order.  
   • Mandatory line format :  
     <IP-address><single space><hit-count>  
     (Example → 192.168.1.50 9)  
   • No header, no trailing blank lines.

2. /home/user/server_logs/summary/error_report.txt  
   • Purpose : report how many ERROR-level messages were logged per calendar day.  
   • Required ordering : chronological ascending (YYYY-MM-DD).  
   • Mandatory line format :  
     YYYY-MM-DD: <error-count>  
     (Example → 2023-10-12: 7)  
   • No header, no trailing blank lines.

3. /home/user/server_logs/summary/avg_cpu_by_server.csv  
   • Purpose : show the *mean* CPU utilisation for each server name that appears in usage.csv.  
   • Required ordering : alphabetical ascending by server name.  
   • Mandatory file must be valid CSV with a single header row exactly:  
     server,avg_cpu_percent  
     The numerical values must be printed with **two digits after the decimal point** (printf-style %.2f).  
     Example final file:  
         server,avg_cpu_percent  
         srv1,38.45  
         srv2,50.11

4. /home/user/server_logs/summary/maintenance_audit.log  
   • Purpose : a one-stop text file embedding the contents of the three previous artefacts in the following, *strict* template (exactly, including the blank lines):  

        [ACCESS]
        <verbatim contents of access_summary.txt>

        [ERRORS]
        <verbatim contents of error_report.txt>

        [CPU]
        <verbatim contents of avg_cpu_by_server.csv without the header line>

   • Make sure there is a single blank line *between* the blocks and **no blank line at the very end of the file**.

Operational constraints:
• Every transformation must rely on awk and/or sed in at least one stage.  
• You are free to use pipelines that combine these with other coreutils (sort, uniq, tee, mkdir, etc.).  
• Do *not* alter the original access.log, error.log or usage.csv files.  
• The agent does not run with root privileges; place every output inside the user’s $HOME path exactly as specified above.

When you are done, you should be able to run the following quick spot-checks successfully (shown here only for your own understanding; **do not** include them in your output):

    $ cat /home/user/server_logs/summary/access_summary.txt
    192.168.1.10 4
    192.168.1.15 4
    192.168.1.20 2
    192.168.1.25 2

    $ cat /home/user/server_logs/summary/error_report.txt
    2023-10-12: 2
    2023-10-13: 2
    2023-10-14: 2

    $ cat /home/user/server_logs/summary/avg_cpu_by_server.csv
    server,avg_cpu_percent
    srv1,37.50
    srv2,51.25
    srv3,22.50

    $ cat /home/user/server_logs/summary/maintenance_audit.log
    [ACCESS]
    192.168.1.10 4
    192.168.1.15 4
    192.168.1.20 2
    192.168.1.25 2

    [ERRORS]
    2023-10-12: 2
    2023-10-13: 2
    2023-10-14: 2

    [CPU]
    srv1,37.50
    srv2,51.25
    srv3,22.50

Hints (not checked by the grader but useful for reasoning):
• Use awk’s associative arrays for counting.  
• For date extraction in error.log, the first 10 characters of the timestamp are sufficient.  
• For usage.csv, skip the header line before aggregating.  
• A single awk program can both accumulate sums and counts and then compute the average in its END block.  

After all four artefacts exist with the correct byte-for-byte content, the task is complete.  The automated evaluation will look for precise file paths, exact line order, whitespace and numeric formatting, so double-check before you finish.
